summary: Stancil argues that clean data is less a solvable engineering problem than a tradeoff that teams often choose, because businesses evolve faster than their data systems can. Tools like SDF help mitigate issues, but they are edge improvements that don’t change the messy, exploratory nature of data work. He suggests shifting from chasing stability to designing for resiliency and easier refactoring.
money_quotes: Companies’ data infrastructures—including those at many young startups—are often terminally messy, full of brittle scripts, manually updated CSVs, and “pipelines” that involve transferring an Excel file from one computer to another with a USB drive.
The mess has a purpose: the development process looks less like an organized construction site and more like an artist’s studio or a writer’s desk.
It’s not a data reliability tool; it’s a data resiliency tool.
The other approach—leaving it all up to Jeff—doesn’t seem to be working.
key_insight: Clean data isn’t a permanent destination; the realistic goal is building systems that tolerate change and make messy realities easier to debug and refactor.
